item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this - it definitely gets my +1! There are a couple minor things that feel a touch too on-the-nose - the 13 rulers and the very last line (though I like the interpretation in the comments that someone from O5 time-travelled).

One nitpick, though. The bit in a footnote about 100 joules of kinetic energy being sufficient to set off a nuclear reaction, or something to that effect? That number is way too low for something like a cannon ball. What you want, as far as I can tell, is essentially something that goes boom when it hits the target, but part of that is *not* going boom *until* it hits the target. If we assume this cannon ball is like a normal iron cannon ball in all regards except for going boom, it simplifies matters - so let’s say it’s a standard cannon ball, and weighs 5.5 kg (about 12 lbs.).

It works out that you would trigger this nuclear reaction just by dropping it three feet. Kinetic energy from the fall is equal to the difference in gravitational potential energy, so energy E = mass times gravitational constant times height difference, or height difference = energy over (mass times gravitational constant). Gravitational constant g is 9.8m/s^2, so if mass is 5.5kg and energy is 100J, that gives us 0.85m, or about 2.8 feet.

Honestly? Simplest fix here is add another zero on, to make it 1000 joules to trigger it. You’re not going to bomb yourself if you drop it, it still goes boom fairly easily, and while a non-anomalous cannon would give it more than enough kick to trigger it by firing it (thus making it go boom all over you), nothing says the delivery mechanism was a literal cannon.

… *cough* me? Overly detailed and disproportionate time investment in the calculations for a response that boils down to ‘add another zero’? It’s more likely than you think!

What can I say?
I really dig the idea (I always had in my "canon" that the Foundation we have today always existed in a way or another through the whole human history, but I didn't thought about an entire kingdom based on it) and how it lead to the modern Foundation.

As someone above, I can't say that I like the explicit references to the modern one (like, the 13 rulers), but rest is pretty cool, in particular the closing line. Yeah, it's even more explicit than the rest, but that works for me.
Having the whole article hinting at "These guys are the Foundation" without being explicit and then "dropping da bomb" with the last line would have been even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1
I had to stop cause of grammar. I can't line by line this whole thing, and it seems some others aleady have. Concept seems ok.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks for the feedback, i will definitely go ahead and make some of the changes that you suggested.

Aside from that, the bull of heaven was meant to just be an ancient equivalent of a keter class skip, not a particular reference

as for the idea of them getting transported back in time, this wasnt my intent. More or less i took the idea of the administrator being immortal to an extreme. i went ahead and included actual blatant mistakes in the structure of the keepers because i wanted it to be clear that it failed through the bad decision making of the administrator. implying that he has built the modern foundation into a powerful and stable organization through practice.

im not trying to persuade you to upvote, but hopefully this clarification helps.

anyways, thanks again for the feedback and or pointing out errors since im a shit-tier proof reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, though the on-the-nose references to the modern Foundation (13 members, the "Purpose" document, the 3 words at the end) make me think this makes more sense as "some members of the present Foundation got transported to the Bronze Age and recreated the Foundation there". I thought the Bull of Heaven was gonna be 682 at first until you mentioned the skeleton.

+ I also noticed a lot of typos, or places where a style change would be helpful.
– hide block

Local governments have been informed of SCP-4015's existence

SCP-4015 has been found to be culturally consistent with the contemporary nomadic Arabian tribes that existed along its borders, however it was noticeably influenced culturally by the urban civilizations to its north in the Greater Levant. Linguistically, SCP-4015 seems to have used Babylonian language in its writings in order to better communicate with its neighboring states.

"although its culture was also influenced by the urban civilizations to its north" sounds better, and "SCP-4015 used the Babylonian language in its writings" reads more clearly.

Literate individuals in SCP-4015, along with those in other Levantine civilizations in the Levant, were known to only refer to the civilization only as "The Keepers". However, in some cases foreign cultures were known to refer to SCP-4015 as "The Heretics."

SCP-4015, for the most part, lacked the urbanization associated with other Bronze Age political entities at this time. However, In spite of its sparse population however, it was a highly unified and organized society. SCP-4015 was a literate society which possessed knowledge of mathematics, physics, chemistry, and medicine far beyond its contemporary societies neighbors. Moreover, translations of preserved documents suggests that, for the duration of its history, SCP-4015 was ruled by a single continuous ruling council for the duration of its history.

They are recorded to have looted temples, killed priests, and to steal stolen religious artifacts.

all religious items acquired through SCP-4015's military activities

Indeed, SCP-4015 also possessed technology beyond the scope of all other Bronze Age civilizations. Among these are its settlements, which were confined to small fortresses never exceeding a few thousand citizens. They were however, extremely sustainable and efficient, however, having been built for long term habitation. Its military technology was also on par with later Iron Age kingdoms rather than its contemporaries , likely explaining. This likely is why it was able to defeat every military opponent it faced.

SCP-4015's distinctive pattern of settlement shows that each settlement was organized around the containment of certain anomalies

Maybe replace the second "settlement" with "site" or "fortress"?

Below is a Document recovered from Site-1/4015 detailing the containment of what was considered an extremely dangerous anomaly by SCP-4015's rulers.

"Document" doesn't need to be capitalized unless it's part of a designation (like "Document 4015-1")

The beast is to be sealed 3 [units?] underground such that it cannot open a hole to the outside, a fortress must protect the entrance to its prison. It is to have each of its legs bound by iron chains and a rope is to be tied to its tail such that its tail is always being pulled. A strong metal mask is to be put on the creature to diminish its destructive gales. The gate to its chamber must be guarded by more than a dozen soldiers at all times, if it must be killed, a single guard is to use the sword recovered from Uruk to cut its back.

This could be explained by having it be originally in Babylonian, but some of these sentences would sound better split up. For instance, "a fortress must protect the entrance to its prison" and "if it must be killed, a single guard is to use the sword recovered from Uruk to cut its back" work perfectly well as complete sentences on their own.

Two cisterns have been dug so as too to flood the Bull's chamber should efforts to stop it fail. Ideally this should drown the creature, however based on past experiences, the bull will return to life several days after drowning.

Again, the bit after "Ideally this should drown the creature" could be split off into a separate sentence.

The site also served as the first hard evidence of direct SCP-4015 involvement in containing anomalies, since then over ███ ancient containment sites have been recovered.

This sounds better if you replace the comma with a period.

It appears that the Capital of SCP-4015 (maybe put "Now designated Site-1/4015" in a footnote or parentheses) was a city-like settlement located at the heart of its proper territory which has been designated as Site-1/4015.

Of note are thirteen especially large fortified houses that likely served as a residence for the rulers of SCP-4015. The houses are clustered around a large gathering space that likely served as the capital building of the government.

This seems risky, putting all 13 members of the ruling council in one place. There's a reason the Foundation doesn't do it. (Of course, this could be a factor in SCP-4015's destruction)

The following document details the initial findings and thoughts regarding an excavation performed under Doctor Hertz, which yielded evidence suggesting that SCP-4015 and Site-1/4015 were likely destroyed by an anomaly whose abilities exceeded their ability to contain it.

A comma after "Doctor Hertz" makes this flow more smoothly.

Its water management system was incredibly advanced, it was able to store and distribute large amounts of potable water to the population of the site through a series of underground pipes and cisterns.

You could split the sentence after "incredibly advanced".

Chemical analysis of the system shows that it might have actually been an attempt at sewage treatment by the population of SCP-4015 in and attempt to conserve, and possibly reuse water.

This is likely an attempt at circumnavigating the lack of arable land surrounding the site.

I think you mean "circumventing"? Circumnavigating is traveling around something (usually the Earth).

These structures have what has been dubbed an "assembly room," which is a large space with unusual equipment that presumably was used for the production of various sorts of weaponry on the site.

Whats What is unusual about the site other than being another its display of the SCP-4015's impressive technological capabilities of SCP-4015 was is its destruction. Hume analysis allows us to conclude (maybe "indicates") that there was an unusual spike in anomalous activity around the Bronze Age Collapse. However there are no sites in the ancient Middle East that show evidence of being destroyed destruction via an extra-normal event. Site-1/4015 is the first site to provide us evidence for anomalous damage.

While the site is actually quite intact, its it is clear that most of the site's inhabitants were killed.

Were they killed in its destruction? Maybe clarify that.

Several kilometers outside of the site we find an ancient blast crater with trace amounts of SCP-████, indicating that they the inhabitants of SCP-4015 used their primitive explosive weapons for defense of the site. While we do not know what its their target was, we do know that it they failed to kill it.

You say we don't know what their target was, but then a few lines later it seems like it's clear that they were firing at the advancing army. Were they not?

In fact, only a small stockpile of SCP-████ was recovered from Site-1/4015. Based on this it was clearly scarce and rarely used, indicating that the military of SCP-4015 had likely exhausted nearly all other options for its defense.

This bit might go better at the end of the paragraph where you introduce the "cannon balls".

Based on weapon findings, we can conclude that a large battle took place a few hundred meters outside of the settlement's walls, in the same direction as the blast crater.

There were not just two opposing armies present., rather Instead, we find that the army forces that engaged SCP-4015 was were a coalition, carrying weapons and armor from all over the Levant. This was a coalition. We find Assyrians, Akkadians, even Amorites fighting side by side. We also found Egyptian chariots and Scythian short swords. We find individuals in Akkadian armor with weapons made from a reddish variety of bronze that we traced all the way to China, and genetic testing shows that these same individuals were originally from Crete. Amazingly, the vulnerability of SCP-4015 was enough to unite several major warring states in the Middle East, moreover, and across the world. On nearly every non-SCP-4015 corpse we find a charm dedicated to Nergal. Based on this, its it is likely that the kingdoms in contact with SCP-4015 finally found a way to relinquish themselves of their supposedly heretical influence.

Is the last "their" about 4015 or its enemies? Also, "liberate" would probably be a better word than "relinquish".
Your footnote about Nergal should be linked to here, the first time you mention him.

Indeed, the corpses of nearly every individual present at the site were in excellent health. As one of our researchers put it "Its It's as if they simply dropped dead."

ending their rule in the east.

Do you mean Middle East?

So that everyone, farmer and king alike, can rest under the illusion that the sun shall rise again tomorrow.

"Illusion" makes it sound like the sun isn't going to rise. Maybe "expectation"?

As detailed above, Site-1/4015, along with the rest of SCP-4015, was destroyed suddenly during the Bronze Age Collapse. During this period Foundation archaeologists have concluded that a rapid upsurge in anomalous activity occurred across the Middle East during this period. The latest known documents from SCP-4015 come from this period. The most important of which seems to have been written by a high ranking official who appears to have established control over SCP-4015 after the dissolution of the ruling council in its final years.

The last two sentences should be merged. Also, maybe add an approximate date for the Bronze Age Collapse?

soon it seems, all of civilization shall be destroyed.

The comma here is unneccessary.

– hide block

No vote for now, but it has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This object class but…I like the skip. Dying Foundation brave against gods and beliefs in the era of the dawn of civilization.Everything engraved in the tragedy and the history can not be reversed, it radiated hope, reason, light and the stupid persistence that did not want to compromise

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, dont worry i understand your criticism. Thing is, I highly doubt that its going to ever be used by someone else and if you think it invalidates the article then I honestly dont mind. while it could pass for safe I wanted to emphasize that this wasnt anomalous but rather only existed under anomalous circumstances, and in that sense I created it for narrative purposes. If you arent satisfied by that justification though…then oh well, I tried lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Making an object class for fun doesn't really justify its existence. If any, it does the complete opposite and makes the article completely pointless.

This may come off as vehement but, if you're going to make an object class, at least have a valid reason for having one, like Ticonderoga or Hiemal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly for fun. I'm well aware that it probably hurts the article overall, but I decided that since it wouldn't have a big impact on the reception that there wasn't any harm in adding it.

So I suppose the rule of cool is the reason that I made the object class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly don't see why we have this object class. We already have 139 and it pretty much describes the class in this article perfectly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like Foundation Precursors are low hanging fruit, especially in this contest. It's not a bad article, but it's not something I'd like to see up there next to 3000 and 2000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you're fine, i will take care of that, thanks for pointing it out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"kingdoms in in contact with SCP-4000"

Got an extra "in" there. Sorry but I'm too chicken to edit anything out of fear of somehow messing up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I had the creative vision to execute that, but its a coincidence I'm afraid. I had never considered that, but I actually like the potential idea that it's basically Ereshkigals attempt at vengeance on the Keepers so i might do something with that down the road.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 because I upvote anything to do with Mesopotamian mythology, and also this is great, especially for a first SCP.

+ show block
– hide block

It might just be a coincidence, but is there any connection between the Keepers killing Ereshkigal's first husband and being invaded by forces worshiping Ereshkigal's second husband?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback, I will consider what you said when I make my next revision, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. This didn't do a whole lot for me I'm afraid.

We have already had a few "Ancient Versions of the Foundation" in this contest, and whereas something along the lines of Socratic Containment Procedures at least has a neat format screw to go along with the exposition, this plays it straight and as a result, just gets bogged down. As soon as the first document is read the big reveal is given away and while there is an opportunity to get into some fun and inventive details as to how a primitive society deals with problems that even the modern Foundation has difficulty with, instead we are given a brief glimpse at these and are pushed down the line to the various peoples of the middle east overthrowing and destroying the Keepers, which itself is rather dry and doesn't go anywhere for me as a reader.

On top of that, the inclusion here of the new object class seems a to fall flat. I'd argue you could have just used "neutralized" and not lost any of the effects.

-1. A tried and true idea in which the execution wasn't quite there this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback

like previously, i would like to address some things.

+ Things
- Are Currently Being Adressed

While I see your point regarding the imaginary element. It, like several other things in the article hinge on suspension of disbelief, which I clearly broke in your case. So, I'm sorry if it bothers you but I am probably keeping it in cause i think its fun. Albeit I have been aware this whole time that an "imaginary atomic number" is total nonsense and has zero grounding in reality, just did for the kewlness.

also, I like your opinions on some of the phrasing so I will go ahead make some changes to that.

as for the iron age tech, I agree that i should clarify that. However, i should make it clear that their use of "nuclear weapons" is akin to the Foundations use of SCP-500, something that they only used under rare circumstances because of the scarcity/power of the anomaly. For the most part they do indeed make use of conventional warfare.

Also, apologies about that map, my original map had more of Syria in it, and I forgot to revise the article according to my current map. So I will get to fixing that

Finally, i wasn't actually aware of SCP-557 I honestly feel dirty after reading it, they even call themselves "The Keepers" its crazy how well that aligns with my story…but I swear to god…I didn't even know what 557 was lol. That being said it really looks more like ancient people trying to contain an anomaly then an actual foundation predecessor. While I highly doubt I'm going to convince you otherwise, I believe that mine shows my head-cannon for the history of the foundation while 557 only talks about some really old Omani dudes attempt at keeping an anomaly locked up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly agree with the points raised by Soixante Huitard, but wanted to add a few things.

Which is why its anomalous. It doesn't make sense, that's the anomaly.

There's a difference between 'it's paranormal/anomalous' and 'it doesn't make sense'. I could accept 'measuring devices displayed an imaginary number when the element was measured' or 'an 'imaginary' particle was detected in the metal using SCP-X' or 'the metal compels people to refer to the atomic number as imaginary' - all of these rely on something which while obviously not real, could exist in the SCP universe. None of these are necessarily inferred by 'the atomic number is imaginary', though. It's like saying 'the atomic number is potato'. There could be an anomalous element where describing the number of protons in it as 'potato' would be logical, but anyone reading that line in a report without further context would just assume it was nonsense. I only harp on this point because I would actually read an SCP about an element with an imaginary atomic number, while this does a disservice to an interesting idea.

+ show block
– hide block

In addition to the Bronze Age/Middle East capitalisation problems, 'iron age' is also not capitalised, and there are a few other grammar problems (I spotted a stray its/it's at least once). While not strictly mistakes, there are a lot of phrasing choices that read oddly to me.

SCP-4000 for the most part, lacked the urbanization

Such as this - I really feel it would read better as 'Mostly, SCP-4000 lacked…' or 'SCP-4000, for the most part,…'. Minor, but this happened enough times to be frustrating by the end.

Due to the nature of SCP-4000, access to knowledge regarding the object has been deemed unnecessary for individuals that are not directly connected with its research or containment process.

I feel like this could apply to literally every SCP.

Describing the military technology as on par with Iron Age kingdoms and then talking about their nuclear weapons seems… wrong.

Extremely minor, but if that tiny sliver of Syria is enough to describe 'southern Syria' as part of its territory, Jordan should be as well.

My main issue with this, though, is that there are a LOT of SCPs that presuppose the existence of an ancient Foundation. SCP-557 is probably the oldest and the first one I thought of. Without much more fleshing out of the ancient Foundation civilisation, this doesn't bring anything new to the table, in spite of being more wordy.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks for the kinda words, but yeah the vertical farming is mostly me using the rule of cool to let them do interesting things.

there is essentially no way an actual Bronze Age vertical farm would ever be viable…but its cool so…yeah

that said i do like to think they have some anomalous technology at play, or just some anachronistic technology, but i probably wont be elaborating on that for the foreseeable future

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this is a ballsy, and relatively well-executed, attempt at a first! If It had to criticize anything it would be a one of the things you added to try and demonstrate how technologically advanced this civilization was.

While the vertical farming bit seems neat, even modern attempts at vertical farming are hindered by problems of economic viability/efficiency and quantitative output. The lighting problem is also fairly challenging even with modern technology like high efficiency LEDs. Mirrors can't actually solve this problem for you since they only reflect the amount of light that actually strikes its small surface (it's also worth mentioning that the sun is a pain since it changes angles on you throughout the day), and for lack of a focusing mechanism there would be substantial losses for each subsequent reflection on top of that (which is why that famous scene in "The Mummy" with the mirrors is b.s).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey! thanks for the criticism, I will fix those grammatical errors you pointed out, so thanks.

While I don't intend on changing your mind about the skip, I would like to address some of the things you brought up

+ Show things
- Hide this shit

- Correct me if I'm misunderstanding this, but I don't think "dating from the late bronze age to the mid-twelfth century BCE" makes sense, because the late Bronze Age was around 1000 BCE. Do you mean "mid-12th century BCE to late Bronze Age"?

So, The late bronze age would have been c. 1500 BC. while the Bronze Age Collapse happened sometime during the 1100s BC. In the middle east that transition to iron weaponry is believed to have occurred sometime around the late 1200s BC. So, my chronology works unless Wikipedia (whom i got my chronology from) is wrong.

- There has to be some term more clinical than "international language". Lingua Franca, maybe.

While its totally subjective, i actually considered that, but decided that Lingua Franca would be less clinical, but i will consider changing this.

- culturally consistent with Arabian nomadic tribes (Bedouins? Not sure who this refers to), influenced by civilizations in the greater Levant (which ones? How?) Needs to be more fleshed out. I get that this refers to a predecessor to the foundation, but you're telling me it's an entire civilization. That should be given more attention.

Honestly, the focus of the article isn't their culture, and I would likely spend too much time elaborating on something that isnt central to the plot if i did that. However, that being said, my intention was to not leave people in the dark as far as this groups culture went, after all it becomes hard to imagine people without associating them with some culture. As the Leader says in the final document, he basically approached some Arabian tribesmen living near the Sumerian frontier and (for vague reasons) they accepted his proposition to assimilate elements of their culture

Now, i was going on my historical knowledge when i wrote this, and iirc we don't really know what the northern Arabian nomads called themselves, but they had lots of shared cultural elements. Perhaps i could say that they were culturally consistent with Semites, but as far as i know that was only one group of this larger culture.

What does it even mean for something to have an imaginary number for an atomic number? That's nonsensical.

Which is why its anomalous. It doesn't make sense, that's the anomaly.

And why does it undergo a nuclear explosion when hit with "sufficient kinetic force"? Why is that not defined, if it's important enough to include?

Because its not necessary to the article itself. I concede that I only added it for fun, not because I thought it would improve that article.

That said, you ignore the fact that, in-universe the guy reading the 4k proposal has no reason to know what "sufficient kinetic force" means, all he needs to know to understand the skip is that, clearly they knew that they could hit the material really hard, and have it explode.

- General clinical tone (example; "amazingly" is probably not good phrasing, "we dubbed" should be something like "informally referred to as", etc.) Is real bad. Lots of informal language that reads more like dialogue than an official report. And the use of the first person is jarring.

Perhaps I didn't make it clear enough that this isn't an official report as much as it was the initial reaction of an archaeologist. So I will give you this one, and I will revise the article to make this clear. Also, in line with my previous statement, the use of first person was intentional.

But yes, the fact that it was essentially some dudes reaction to the info collected after a series of excavations is mean to explain his tone here.

- minor, but nuclear weapons? Weaponized lazers? Why? Why on Earth? What does that contribute to the narrative?

I will have to invoke the rule of cool here, but i do believe I have a valid reason for doing this. Its meant to show how their Foundation-esque attempts at studying an analyzing anomalies, as later stated in "Purpose" allowed them to not only use technological anomalies that were well beyond their own capabilities, but also that they were frequently faced with high-tech anomalies.

It also is meant to show the sheer variety of destructive things that escaped their containment during the bronze age collapse

Mechanical problems aside, the concept is underwhelming. This foundation predecessor isn't really given any depth, all the info in the article is just generic mission statement stuff that closely parallels the Foundation proper. It's not compelling to read another rehashing of the same paragraph-length explanation about "dying in darkness so you can live in the light". -1

I don't think i can do anything to convince you that the predecessor has depth (although, no doubt, it could be better developed had i added more supplementary information/documents, although I don't believe that I am talented enough to make the article NOT drag on after a certain point.)

The idea however was to show how spiritually close the modern Foundation is to the Keepers, so yes i intentionally paralleled them heavily.

As for you last statement, I don't think that my last document was a rehash of "dying in darkness so you can live in the light" (cause I honestly think its cringe and melodramatic). It has more or less The Leader lamenting his own actions, before resolving to continue his mission in spite of the likelihood that he will fail again. Not some long-winded explanation about the sacrifices that people make for the Foundation, which I don't address here.

So yes, thanks for your opinion, I quite like some of your suggestions, so I will likely do a revision soon to make some minor fixes/improvements. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- Bronze Age and Middle East should be capitalized throughout

- Correct me if I'm misunderstanding this, but I don't think "dating from the late bronze age to the mid-twelfth century BCE" makes sense, because the late Bronze Age was around 1000 BCE. Do you mean "mid-12th century BCE to late Bronze Age"?

- There has to be some term more clinical than "international language". Lingua Franca, maybe.

- culturally consistent with Arabian nomadic tribes (Bedouins? Not sure who this refers to), influenced by civilizations in the greater Levant (which ones? How?) Needs to be more fleshed out. I get that this refers to a predecessor to the foundation, but you're telling me it's an entire civilization. That should be given more attention.

- everything about "Anomaly in question was an unidentified metal, its "atomic number" corresponded to an imaginary number. When sufficient kinetic force is applied it can rapidly undergo a highly destructive nuclear reaction." is bad. What does it even mean for something to have an imaginary number for an atomic number? That's nonsensical. And why does it undergo a nuclear explosion when hit with "sufficient kinetic force"? Why is that not defined, if it's important enough to include?

- Excavation log has issues with run on sentences

- General clinical tone (example; "amazingly" is probably not good phrasing, "we dubbed" should be something like "informally referred to as", etc.) Is real bad. Lots of informal language that reads more like dialogue than an official report. And the use of the first person is jarring.

- minor, but nuclear weapons? Weaponized lazers? Why? Why on Earth? What does that contribute to the narrative?

Mechanical problems aside, the concept is underwhelming. This foundation predecessor isn't really given any depth, all the info in the article is just generic mission statement stuff that closely parallels the Foundation proper. It's not compelling to read another rehashing of the same paragraph-length explanation about "dying in darkness so you can live in the light". -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo, im down for that. I feel like it has potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, once this contest is over, want to start up a canon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed, just like the Leader predicted, humanity was just a pawn in the game of the gods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have long been fascinated by the Late Bronze Age Collapse, so this was quite a treat. We know going in that they eventually failed and fell, yet it was still moving to read the last entry.

Somehow, I'm guessing that the victorious alliance rapidly realized their mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks, I'm glad you enjoyed it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, that really means a lot to me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty bomb, dude. I could see a series of things written like the bull, goi-format style. And it had been obvious all along that SCP-4000 was similar to the Foundation, but the twist at the end still caught me off-guard.

easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip has some heart. I love how many first skips are entering in this contest and doing successful.

I always love seeing the history of the Foundation fleshed out. This is no exception. Great work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, i posted my first SCP after being here for 5 years, and for the 4k contest no less! Probably not a good idea to shoot for 4k as a first article but…I like risks.

I had a total blast writing this, and hopefully you enjoy reading it as much as I did writing it. I got to talk about history, mythology, and crazy secrets, all things I love.

I am very eager to hear everyone's thoughts about this piece, which are hopefully good, but you can think whatever you want.

I would like to thank Countless Skies, and the folks at the SCPDecalssified Discord for their critique and advice.

The first image was created by yours truly, while the other two are under creative commons, they are all linked here:

https://www.scribblemaps.com/maps/view/thing/WeAelIjyt3

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Mohenjodaro_-_view_of_the_stupa_mound.JPG/1280px-Mohenjodaro_-_view_of_the_stupa_mound.JPG

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Ottosdal1.jpg

Here's some extra info for the curious:

 + Spoilerz
 - You have encountered a wild spoiler

Hopefully the article made it clear, but i would like to clarify some things for anyone who might be confused:

  • SCP-4000 is the earliest incarnation of the foundation, although their link is very small, in a sense SCP-4000 represents the earliest attempt by humans at containing anomalies.
  • “Leader” is the administrator, he is immortal and i wanted to take his immortality to an extreme.
  • The Foundation is actually his latest and most successful attempt at containing the anomalous
  • The “Bull of Heaven” was in a sense and ancient SCP write-up
  • The fact that there were 13 important people plus the administrator shows that he has a lot of faith in the 13 member council-based leadership system of the Foundation.
  • A mass containment breach caused the bronze age collapse, it’s cliche, but I couldn't resist lol.

There are a lot of little historical/mythological references, hopefully you found them and now feel good about yourself. Cause that mean you know a little bit more about Sumerian history than most people!

So that's basically it, thanks!

Edit: I made the article more aesthetically pleasing, big thanks to YossiLeiner for the CSS! Also thanks to Sterbai for pointing out a typo to me and reviewing the article!
